stem: The \( [[n]] \)th roots of unity are \( e^{2\pi i k/[[n]]} \) for \( k = 0, 1, \dots, [[n]]-1 \). Compute the root with \( k = [[k]] \), namely \( e^{2\pi i [[k]]/[[n]]} \), in rectangular form \( x + yi \).
solution: With angle \( \theta = \dfrac{2\pi [[k]]}{[[n]]} \), the root is \( \cos\theta + i\sin\theta = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This swaps sine and cosine; the real part is \( \cos\theta \).
This is the conjugate root \( e^{-2\pi i k/n} \); the sign of the imaginary part was flipped.
This is the diametrically opposite point \( -e^{2\pi i k/n} \).
This rotates the correct root by an extra quarter turn (multiplying by \( i \)).
This is off by one in the real part; every root of unity has modulus 1.
This is off by one in the real part; every root of unity has modulus 1.

stem: Since \( [[a]] = [[b]]^{[[n]]} \), the equation \( z^{[[n]]} = [[a]] \) has \( [[n]] \) complex solutions \( z_k = [[b]]\left(\cos\dfrac{2\pi k}{[[n]]} + i\sin\dfrac{2\pi k}{[[n]]}\right) \) for \( k = 0, 1, \dots, [[n]]-1 \). Compute \( z_{[[k]]} \) in rectangular form \( x + yi \).
solution: Every root has modulus \( \sqrt[[[n]]]{[[a]]} = [[b]] \) (not \( [[a]] \) itself), and argument \( \dfrac{2\pi [[k]]}{[[n]]} \), so \( z_{[[k]]} = [[b]]\left(\cos\dfrac{2\pi [[k]]}{[[n]]} + i\sin\dfrac{2\pi [[k]]}{[[n]]}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This swaps sine and cosine; the real part is \( [[b]]\cos\theta \).
This is the conjugate root, obtained by flipping the sign of the imaginary part.
This is the diametrically opposite point on the circle of radius \( [[b]] \).
This forgets to take the \( [[n]] \)th root of the modulus, using \( [[a]] \) instead of \( [[b]] \) as the radius.
This is off by one from the correct real part.
This is off by one from the correct real part.

stem: Since \( [[a]] = [[b]]^{[[n]]} \), the equation \( z^{[[n]]} = -[[a]] \) has \( [[n]] \) complex solutions \( z_k = [[b]]\left(\cos\dfrac{\pi + 2\pi k}{[[n]]} + i\sin\dfrac{\pi + 2\pi k}{[[n]]}\right) \) for \( k = 0, 1, \dots, [[n]]-1 \) (note the starting angle is \( \pi \), not \( 0 \), since the base is negative). Compute \( z_{[[k]]} \) in rectangular form \( x + yi \).
solution: A negative base contributes a starting argument of \( \pi \) (since \( -[[a]] = [[a]]\,e^{i\pi} \)), so \( z_{[[k]]} = [[b]]\left(\cos\dfrac{\pi+2\pi [[k]]}{[[n]]} + i\sin\dfrac{\pi+2\pi [[k]]}{[[n]]}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This omits the \( \pi \) phase shift, treating \( -[[a]] \) as if it were the positive number \( [[a]] \).
This swaps sine and cosine in the correct angle.
This is the conjugate of the correct root.
This is the diametrically opposite point of the correct root.
This is off by one from the correct real part.
This is off by one from the correct real part.

stem: How many complex numbers are simultaneously an \( [[n]] \)th root of unity and an \( [[m]] \)th root of unity?
solution: A number is a common root exactly when it is a root of unity of order dividing both \( [[n]] \) and \( [[m]] \); the set of such orders is the divisors of \( \gcd([[n]],[[m]]) \), and there are exactly \( \gcd([[n]],[[m]]) = [[answer]] \) common roots (the \( \gcd([[n]],[[m]]) \)th roots of unity).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies \( [[n]] \) and \( [[m]] \) instead of taking their greatest common divisor.
This computes the least common multiple, which counts the combined order, not the number of common roots.
This is the absolute difference of \( [[n]] \) and \( [[m]] \), not their greatest common divisor.
This adds \( [[n]] \) and \( [[m]] \) instead of taking their greatest common divisor.
This is off by one from the correct count.
This doubles the correct count.

stem: The \( [[n]] \)th roots of unity are precisely the \( [[n]] \) roots of \( x^{[[n]]} - 1 = 0 \). Using Vieta's formulas, compute the product of all \( [[n]] \) roots of unity.
solution: For \( x^{[[n]]} - 1 = 0 \), Vieta's formula gives the product of the roots as \( (-1)^{[[n]]}\cdot\dfrac{-1}{1} = (-1)^{[[n]]+1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is correct only when \( [[n]] \) is odd; it drops the sign that depends on the parity of \( [[n]] \).
This is correct only when \( [[n]] \) is even; it drops the sign that depends on the parity of \( [[n]] \).
This confuses the product of the roots with their sum, which is \( 0 \) for \( [[n]] \ge 2 \).
This uses \( [[n]] \) itself rather than the sign pattern \( (-1)^{[[n]]+1} \) from Vieta's formula.
This has the parity of the exponent flipped from the correct formula \( (-1)^{[[n]]+1} \).
The product of roots of unity always has modulus \( 1 \), so it can only be \( \pm 1 \), never \( 2 \).

stem: Let \( w = e^{2\pi i/[[n]]} \) be the primitive \( [[n]] \)th root of unity. Using \( w^{[[n]]} = 1 \) to reduce the exponent, compute \( w^{[[m]]} \) in rectangular form \( x + yi \).
solution: Reduce the exponent modulo \( [[n]] \): \( w^{[[m]]} = w^{[[m]] \bmod [[n]]} = \cos\dfrac{2\pi [[m]]}{[[n]]} + i\sin\dfrac{2\pi [[m]]}{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \) (the angle \( \frac{2\pi [[m]]}{[[n]]} \) only depends on \( [[m]] \) modulo \( [[n]] \)).
distractor_rationales: This swaps sine and cosine; the real part is \( \cos\theta \).
This is the conjugate \( w^{-[[m]]} \); the sign of the imaginary part was flipped.
This is the diametrically opposite point \( -w^{[[m]]} \).
This mistakenly divides the angle by \( [[n]]^2 \) instead of \( [[n]] \).
This is off by one in the real part; every power of a root of unity has modulus 1.
This is off by one in the real part; every power of a root of unity has modulus 1.

stem: Let \( w_m = e^{2\pi i m/[[n]]} \) denote the \( [[n]] \)th roots of unity, the vertices of a regular \( [[n]] \)-gon inscribed in the unit circle. Compute the (straight-line) distance \( |w_{[[j]]} - w_{[[k]]}| \) between the vertices with \( m = [[j]] \) and \( m = [[k]] \).
solution: The straight-line (chord) distance between two points on the unit circle at angles \( \theta_1,\theta_2 \) is \( |e^{i\theta_1}-e^{i\theta_2}| = 2\left|\sin\dfrac{\theta_1-\theta_2}{2}\right| \); evaluating at \( \theta_1 = \tfrac{2\pi [[j]]}{[[n]]}, \theta_2 = \tfrac{2\pi [[k]]}{[[n]]} \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This confuses the vertex index difference with the actual geometric distance.
This computes the arc length along the circle between the two vertices, not the straight-line chord distance.
This compares only the real parts (a projection), not the full complex distance.
This compares only the imaginary parts (a projection), not the full complex distance.
This is off by one from the correct chord distance.
This is half the correct chord distance.
